stimulus: Some astrobiologists searching for signs of extraterrestrial life have proposed that atmospheric CH3Cl (methyl chloride) can serve as a biosignature gas—a marker indicating that a planet may host life. Sukrit Ranjan and colleagues examined this possibility, finding that on rocky planets orbiting cool dwarf stars, CH3Cl produced by oceanic microbes could in principle accumulate to detectable concentrations because such stars emit relatively little ultraviolet radiation, and ultraviolet light is what ordinarily destroys CH3Cl in planetary atmospheres. Around hotter stars, by contrast, the gas would break down too quickly to be a reliable indicator.
stem: Based on the text, Ranjan and colleagues would most likely agree with which statement about atmospheric CH3Cl?

answerOptions.content: It is the most reliable biosignature gas for use in searches for life around any kind of star.
It is more readily produced by oceanic microbes than by terrestrial microbes.
It is unlikely ever to accumulate to detectable levels in the atmosphere of any rocky planet.
Its usefulness as a biosignature depends in part on the type of star a planet orbits.

rationale:
Choice D is the best answer. The text says CH3Cl could accumulate to detectable levels around cool dwarf stars because such stars emit little ultraviolet light, but around hotter stars the gas breaks down too quickly to serve as a reliable indicator. Ranjan and colleagues would thus agree that its usefulness as a biosignature depends on the host star.

Choice A is incorrect because the text says CH3Cl is not reliable around hotter stars. Choice B is incorrect because the text doesn’t compare microbe types. Choice C is incorrect because the text says the gas could in principle accumulate around cool dwarfs.
